medications.indication_rationale: Beta-blockade is the mainstay of long QT syndrome management, blunting the catecholamine surges that trigger Torsades. It is initiated by cardiology/electrophysiology AFTER the diagnosis is confirmed on ECG — the telehealth AI Doctor must NOT start it remotely, and no dose is given here because the source note does not specify one and dosing is specialist- and diagnosis-dependent.
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not initiate before ECG confirmation and specialist review
Standard beta-blocker cautions (e.g. significant asthma/bradycardia) to be assessed by the specialist

medications.drug_name: QT-prolonging medications (e.g. macrolide antibiotics, certain antiemetics, some antidepressants/antipsychotics)
medications.indication_rationale: Any QT-prolonging drug must be AVOIDED in suspected long QT syndrome — it compounds the repolarisation defect and can precipitate fatal Torsades. Prescribing one (a classic error of commission) is dangerous; the patient should be counselled to avoid these and to flag the condition to any future prescriber.

medications.drug_name: Antiepileptic drug (e.g. a benzodiazepine or sodium valproate) started for a presumed seizure
medications.indication_rationale: Treating this as epilepsy and starting an antiepileptic is the red-herring error — the movements are convulsive syncope from an arrhythmia, not a primary seizure. It would delay the cardiac work-up while leaving the lethal substrate untreated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Immediately create a strictly low-stimulus environment — silence all phones, alarms and loud notifications and move away from the sounding building alarm.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removes the exact acoustic/startle trigger that provoked the arrhythmia, preventing an immediate recurrent (potentially fatal) event before hospital arrival.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Maintain a personal list of drugs to avoid (QT-prolonging medicines) and declare the suspected condition to every future prescriber and pharmacist.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents iatrogenic QT prolongation, a common and avoidable cause of Torsades in these patients.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid strenuous/competitive exercise, swimming and sudden startle exposure until cleared by a cardiologist.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Adrenergic surges (exertion, swimming, startle) are recognised arrhythmia triggers in long QT syndrome; activity restriction reduces risk pending specialist assessment.

patient_education_points.point: Fainting suddenly from a loud noise, especially with your family history, can point to a problem with the heart's electrical 'recharging' rather than a simple faint — an urgent heart tracing (ECG) in hospital is needed to check this and keep you safe.
This is an electrical issue, not a structural one, so a heart that looks and feels normal can still have it — that is exactly why the ECG matters.
These conditions carry a real risk but are highly manageable once diagnosed — with specific medicines (beta-blockers), avoiding certain drugs, and avoiding triggers.
Because it can run in families, close relatives may need checking once your diagnosis is confirmed.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate acute hospital assessment/admission now; cardiology/electrophysiology and clinical-genetics follow-up after discharge
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: 12-lead ECG QTc, continuous rhythm monitoring, electrolytes and echocardiogram; then specialist confirmation, beta-blocker initiation, trigger-avoidance plan and family screening
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any recurrence of fainting, near-fainting, palpitations or collapse — call 000 immediately and commence CPR if unresponsive/not breathing normally

must_include_items: Recognise this as a high-risk (possible cardiac/arrhythmic) syncope, NOT a benign faint or a primary seizure
Escalate for an urgent 12-lead ECG and cardiac monitoring via a monitored emergency ambulance (000) — do not advise self-transport
Immediately institute trigger avoidance: silence all alarms/phones and keep the environment quiet
Advise the patient to stay lying or sitting and not to stand or walk
Instruct the roommate to call 000 back and start CPR if the patient collapses and is unresponsive/not breathing normally
acceptable_alternatives: If the patient refuses an ambulance, strongly counsel against driving and arrange the most urgent monitored in-person cardiac assessment achievable, documenting the risk
errors_of_omission: Failing to escalate for an urgent ECG / cardiac assessment
Failing to advise trigger avoidance while the alarm is still sounding
Failing to weight the family history of young sudden death
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient it was a simple vasovagal faint and discharging without an ECG
Diagnosing epilepsy and referring to neurology / starting an antiepileptic
Being falsely reassured by a structurally normal heart and clearing the patient
Recommending or prescribing a QT-prolonging medication
Advising the patient to drive themselves to hospital
Attempting to start a beta-blocker remotely before ECG confirmation and specialist review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising a high-risk syncope and escalating for urgent ECG/monitored transport (000). Labelling it a benign faint or a seizure, or clearing it remotely, is a critical under-triage failure regardless of other content.
